1.ESV:
1. How lonely sits the city that was full of people! How like a widow has she become, she who was great among the nations! She who was a princess among the provinces has become a slave.
2. She weeps bitterly in the night, with tears on her cheeks; among all her lovers she has none to comfort her; all her friends have dealt treacherously with her; they have become her enemies.
3. Judah has gone into exile because of affliction and hard servitude; she dwells now among the nations, but finds no resting place; her pursuers have all overtaken her in the midst of her distress.
4. The roads to Zion mourn, for none come to the festival; all her gates are desolate; her priests groan; her virgins have been afflicted, and she herself suffers bitterly.
5. Her foes have become the head; her enemies prosper, because the LORD has afflicted her for the multitude of her transgressions; her children have gone away, captives before the foe.
6. From the daughter of Zion all her majesty has departed. Her princes have become like deer that find no pasture; they fled without strength before the pursuer.
7. Jerusalem remembers in the days of her affliction and wandering all the precious things that were hers from days of old. When her people fell into the hand of the foe, and there was none to help her, her foes gloated over her; they mocked at her downfall.
8. Jerusalem sinned grievously; therefore she became filthy; all who honored her despise her, for they have seen her nakedness; she herself groans and turns her face away.
9. Her uncleanness was in her skirts; she took no thought of her future; therefore her fall is terrible; she has no comforter. “O LORD, behold my affliction, for the enemy has triumphed!”
10. The enemy has stretched out his hands over all her precious things; for she has seen the nations enter her sanctuary, those whom you forbade to enter your congregation.
11. All her people groan as they search for bread; they trade their treasures for food to revive their strength. “Look, O LORD, and see, for I am despised.”
12. “Is it nothing to you, all you who pass by? Look and see if there is any sorrow like my sorrow, which was brought upon me, which the LORD inflicted on the day of his fierce anger.
13. “From on high he sent fire; into my bones he made it descend; he spread a net for my feet; he turned me back; he has left me stunned, faint all the day long.
14. “My transgressions were bound into a yoke; by his hand they were fastened together; they were set upon my neck; he caused my strength to fail; the Lord gave me into the hands of those whom I cannot withstand.
15. “The Lord rejected all my mighty men in my midst; he summoned an assembly against me to crush my young men; the Lord has trodden as in a winepress the virgin daughter of Judah.
16. “For these things I weep; my eyes flow with tears; for a comforter is far from me, one to revive my spirit; my children are desolate, for the enemy has prevailed.”
17. Zion stretches out her hands, but there is none to comfort her; the LORD has commanded against Jacob that his neighbors should be his foes; Jerusalem has become a filthy thing among them.
18. “The LORD is in the right, for I have rebelled against his word; but hear, all you peoples, and see my suffering; my young women and my young men have gone into captivity.
19. “I called to my lovers, but they deceived me; my priests and elders perished in the city, while they sought food to revive their strength.
20. “Look, O LORD, for I am in distress; my stomach churns; my heart is wrung within me, because I have been very rebellious. In the street the sword bereaves; in the house it is like death.
21. “They heard my groaning, yet there is no one to comfort me. All my enemies have heard of my trouble; they are glad that you have done it. You have brought the day you announced; now let them be as I am.
22. “Let all their evildoing come before you, and deal with them as you have dealt with me because of all my transgressions; for my groans are many, and my heart is faint.”

1.notes:
Lamentations - Chapter 1
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
Jerusalem sits desolate like a widow who was once a queen among nations. Her lovers have betrayed her, her gates are empty, her priests groan. She weeps bitterly in the night with no comforter. The city herself cries out: "Is it nothing to you, all ye that pass by? behold, and see if there be any sorrow like unto my sorrow." Sin brought this devastation, and God is righteous in His judgment.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Traditionally Jeremiah (the "weeping prophet"), written
after Jerusalem's fall in 586 BC. The Septuagint (Greek OT) and
Jewish tradition both attribute it to Jeremiah, and the anguished
tone matches his prophetic writings. He witnessed the destruction
firsthand.

Classification: Poetic Lament (Acrostic)
Key Themes: The desolation of Jerusalem, the absence of comfort,
the consequences of persistent sin, God's righteous judgment,
the city personified as a weeping woman, loneliness and shame
after judgment

Historical Context: Jerusalem fell to Nebuchadnezzar in 586 BC
after an 18-month siege. The temple was burned, the walls
demolished, the population either killed, starved, or deported.
Lamentations was likely written in the immediate aftermath —
while the ruins still smoldered. The emotion is raw, unfiltered,
fresh grief.

Chapter 1 is an ACROSTIC poem: each of the 22 verses begins with
a successive letter of the Hebrew alphabet (aleph through tav).
This literary structure suggests deliberate, crafted grief — not
random wailing but ORDERED lament. The acrostic form says: "I
will mourn from A to Z, covering EVERYTHING." Grief that is
structured enough to write poetry but honest enough to sob.

The city is personified as a woman — specifically a WIDOW (v.1)
and an UNCLEAN woman (v.8-9). She who was "great among the
nations" and "princess among the provinces" now sits alone,
tributary, abandoned. Her "lovers" (the nations she trusted
instead of God — Egypt, Assyria) have all betrayed her. There
is NO comforter — the phrase appears three times (vv.2,9,17).

Structure:
- Jerusalem the Widow: Alone and Weeping (vv.1-7)
- Jerusalem's Sin and Shame Exposed (vv.8-11)
- The City Speaks: "Is It Nothing to You?" (vv.12-16)
- No Comforter; God is Righteous (vv.17-22)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Isaiah      | 47      | 7-9                | Babylon too would become "a widow" — the same fate awaiting every proud city
v.2              | Psalm       | 137     | 1                  | "By the rivers of Babylon, there we sat down, yea, we wept" — the same grief from the exiles' perspective
v.8              | Deuteronomy | 28      | 15,37              | The covenant curses: "thou shalt become an astonishment" — fulfilled here
v.12             | Isaiah      | 53      | 3                  | "a man of sorrows, and acquainted with grief" — Christ's unmatched sorrow
v.12             | Matthew     | 27      | 39                 | Those passing by the cross wagging their heads — "Is it nothing to you?"
v.15             | Isaiah      | 63      | 3                  | "I have trodden the winepress alone" — God treading in judgment
v.18             | Nehemiah    | 9       | 33                 | "thou art just in all that is brought upon us; for thou hast done right, but we have done wickedly."
v.21             | Psalm       | 86      | 17                 | "that they which hate me may see it, and be ashamed."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/lamentations-1/

Charles Spurgeon:
  "'Is it nothing to you, all ye that pass by?' The desolate city
  asks — and the crucified Christ asks still. Men walk past
  Calvary with empty eyes and busy schedules. They pass the Cross
  as tourists pass ruins — curious, perhaps, but unmoved. Stop,
  sinner! LOOK at what your sin has done. See the sorrow your
  rebellion caused. It should be EVERYTHING to you."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "How" (KJV/ESV) — Hebrew "'Ekhah" = How! / Alas! This word is the Hebrew title of the entire book ("Ekhah"). It's a cry of astonishment and grief — not a question seeking information but an exclamation of disbelief. "HOW could this happen to us?"
v.1              | KJV         | "solitary" (KJV) / "lonely" (ESV) — Hebrew "badad" = alone, isolated, separated. The same word used of the scapegoat sent into the wilderness (Leviticus 16:22). Jerusalem is now the scapegoat — bearing sin's consequences in isolation.
v.8              | KJV         | "removed" (KJV) / "become filthy" (ESV) — Hebrew "nidah" = ceremonial impurity, specifically menstrual uncleanness. A shocking, intimate image: Jerusalem is unclean like a woman in her impurity — people shrink back from touching her. Once a bride; now untouchable.
v.12             | KJV         | "all ye that pass by" (KJV/ESV) — Hebrew "kol overey derekh" = all who cross the road/path. This is the cry of someone on the side of the road — collapsed, visible, yet IGNORED. The suffering is public but the compassion is absent.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
No Comforter      | Repeated three times in chapter 1 (vv.2,9,17). The absence of comfort intensifies grief. None of Jerusalem's former allies offer aid. Only God can comfort — and for now, He is the one afflicting. | 
Acrostic Structure | Each verse begins with the next letter of the Hebrew alphabet (22 letters = 22 verses). Structured grief: "I will mourn completely, from A to Z, leaving nothing unlamented." | 
Princess Become Tributary | Hebrew "sarah" (princess/ruler) become "mas" (forced labor/slave). The reversal of status is total: from ruling to serving, from commanding to obeying. Sin always demotes. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Is it nothing to you?" (v.12). The suffering of others demands our
   attention. Who around you is sitting in ruins while the world passes by?
   Stop. Look. Enter their grief. And before the Cross — STOP. Let it mean
   everything.

2. "She remembered not her last end" (v.9). Sin blinds to consequences. In
   the moment of rebellion, you never think about the morning after.
   Consider your "last end" NOW — while there's still time to choose
   differently.

3. "The LORD is righteous" (v.18). Even in the wreckage, God is right. Can
   you say this in YOUR devastation? Not "God is unfair" but "God is
   righteous, and I brought this on myself." Honest confession is the first
   step toward restoration.

4. "No comforter" (vv.2,9,17). Sometimes you will find yourself in a season
   with NO human comfort. Friends fail, family disappoints, allies
   disappear. In those seasons, you discover whether God alone is enough. He
   promises to be the "God of all comfort" (2 Corinthians 1:3) — even when
   human comforters vanish.

5. Grief can be structured and STILL be genuine (acrostic form). You don't
   have to choose between processed and passionate. Writing your pain,
   ordering your grief, bringing structure to your tears — this is healthy,
   holy lamenting. Lament well.

------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Lamentations Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/lamentations/

David Guzik — Lamentations 1 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/lamentations-1/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

2.ESV:
1. How the Lord in his anger has set the daughter of Zion under a cloud! He has cast down from heaven to earth the splendor of Israel; he has not remembered his footstool in the day of his anger.
2. The Lord has swallowed up without mercy all the habitations of Jacob; in his wrath he has broken down the strongholds of the daughter of Judah; he has brought down to the ground in dishonor the kingdom and its rulers.
3. He has cut down in fierce anger all the might of Israel; he has withdrawn from them his right hand in the face of the enemy; he has burned like a flaming fire in Jacob, consuming all around.
4. He has bent his bow like an enemy, with his right hand set like a foe; and he has killed all who were delightful in our eyes; in the tent of the daughter of Zion, he has poured out his fury like fire.
5. The Lord has become like an enemy; he has swallowed up Israel; he has swallowed up all its palaces; he has laid in ruins its strongholds, and he has multiplied in the daughter of Judah mourning and lamentation.
6. He has laid waste his booth like a garden, laid in ruins his meeting place; the LORD has made Zion forget festival and Sabbath, and in his fierce indignation has spurned king and priest.
7. The Lord has scorned his altar, disowned his sanctuary; he has delivered into the hand of the enemy the walls of her palaces; they raised a clamor in the house of the LORD as on the day of festival.
8. The LORD determined to lay in ruins the wall of the daughter of Zion; he stretched out the measuring line; he did not restrain his hand from destroying; he caused rampart and wall to lament; they languished together.
9. Her gates have sunk into the ground; he has ruined and broken her bars; her king and princes are among the nations; the law is no more, and her prophets find no vision from the LORD.
10. The elders of the daughter of Zion sit on the ground in silence; they have thrown dust on their heads and put on sackcloth; the young women of Jerusalem have bowed their heads to the ground.
11. My eyes are spent with weeping; my stomach churns; my bile is poured out to the ground because of the destruction of the daughter of my people, because infants and babies faint in the streets of the city.

2.notes:
Lamentations - Chapter 2
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
God Himself is revealed as the destroyer of Jerusalem — not merely permitting destruction but actively pouring out His wrath. "The Lord was as an enemy: he hath swallowed up Israel." Every institution fails: the temple burned, the law silenced, the prophets finding no vision from God. Children faint in the streets. Mothers weep over starving infants. The chapter closes with a raw cry to God as children die in their mothers' arms.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Traditionally Jeremiah (the "weeping prophet"), written
after Jerusalem's fall in 586 BC. Jeremiah was present during
the siege and destruction, making him an eyewitness to the
horrors described here.

Classification: Poetic Lament (Acrostic)
Key Themes: God as active agent of judgment (not merely passive
observer), the destruction of all institutions (temple, law,
prophecy), the failure of false prophets, the horror of siege
conditions, the appropriateness of grief

Historical Context: Chapter 2 shifts the focus from Jerusalem's
grief (ch.1) to GOD's action. The theological shock is
deliberate: fourteen times in the first eight verses, GOD is the
subject of destructive verbs. He swallowed, He destroyed, He cut
off, He burned, He bent His bow, He killed, He poured out. This
is not enemy action — this is DIVINE action. The Babylonians were
merely the instrument; God was the hand that wielded them.

This chapter also serves as a acrostic poem (22 verses, 22 Hebrew
letters), though notably with a slight variation: in chapters 2-4
the letters PE and AYIN are reversed from their traditional order.
This may reflect the disorder that judgment brings — even the
alphabet is disrupted.

The false prophets receive particular condemnation (v.14): they
saw "false and deceptive visions" and "did not expose your
iniquity." They told the people what they wanted to hear instead
of what they needed to hear. Their failure to preach repentance
directly contributed to the catastrophe. True prophets (like
Jeremiah) warned for decades and were ignored or persecuted.

Structure:
- God as the Destroyer: "He Hath Swallowed Up" (vv.1-9)
- The Silence: No Vision, No Law, No Word (v.9)
- The Elders Sit on the Ground in Silence (v.10)
- Children Dying in the Streets (vv.11-12)
- "What Can I Say?" — The Incomparability of Suffering (v.13)
- The False Prophets' Failure (v.14)
- Enemies Mocking (vv.15-16)
- "The LORD Hath Done What He Purposed" (v.17)
- Cry to the Lord: Children Fainting (vv.18-19)
- The Final Horror: Mothers and Children (vv.20-22)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Isaiah      | 14      | 12                 | "How art thou fallen from heaven, O Lucifer" — the same "How!" (ekhah) of astonished lamentation
v.5              | Jeremiah    | 30      | 14                 | "I have wounded thee with the wound of an enemy... for the multitude of thine iniquity."
v.6              | Psalm       | 74      | 7                  | "They have cast fire into thy sanctuary."
v.9              | Amos        | 8       | 11                 | "a famine... of hearing the words of the LORD." Prophetic silence as judgment
v.9              | Psalm       | 74      | 9                  | "We see not our signs: there is no more any prophet."
v.14             | Jeremiah    | 23      | 16-17              | "They speak a vision of their own heart, and not out of the mouth of the LORD."
v.14             | Ezekiel     | 13      | 10                 | "they have seduced my people, saying, Peace; and there was no peace."
v.17             | Deuteronomy | 28      | 15-68              | The covenant curses — all fulfilled in the destruction of 586 BC

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/lamentations-2/

Charles Spurgeon:
  "The Lord was 'as an enemy' — terrible words! When God turns
  His hand against a people, no fortress holds. No alliance
  helps. No army resists. And yet — AS an enemy, not IS an
  enemy. Even in wrath He remembers mercy. The surgeon's knife
  feels like an enemy's blade, but its purpose is healing. God
  wounds to cure. He breaks to mend. But oh, the breaking is
  severe."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "cast down from heaven" (KJV) / "cast down from heaven" (ESV) — Hebrew "hishlikh mishamayim" = to hurl from heaven to earth. The imagery is violent: God took Jerusalem's exalted position and HURLED it downward. The verb suggests force, velocity, deliberate action.
v.5              | KJV         | "as an enemy" (KJV/ESV) — Hebrew "ke-oyev" = like/as an enemy. The preposition "ke" (like/as) is theologically critical: it indicates COMPARISON, not identity. God acts in a way that RESEMBLES enemy action but IS disciplinary action. The distinction matters: enemies seek destruction; God seeks restoration.
v.13             | KJV         | "breach" (KJV) / "ruin" (ESV) — Hebrew "shever" = breaking, fracture, crushing. Related to "shavar" (to break). "Great like the sea" — the image is of a dam break: unstoppable, overwhelming, all-consuming. The destruction is oceanic in scale.
v.17             | KJV         | "devised" (KJV) / "planned" (ESV) — Hebrew "zamam" = to plan, purpose, determine. This destruction was not impulsive divine rage — it was PLANNED, purposed, determined long ago. God warned in Deuteronomy, through Isaiah, through Jeremiah. He "fulfilled" what He said He would do.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
God as Enemy      | The devastating theological reality of chapter 2: God Himself is the active destroyer. Not Satan, not random evil, but the sovereign Lord executing covenantal judgment. "As an enemy" preserves the distinction between wrath and hatred. | 
Prophetic Silence | When God withdraws the prophetic word as an act of judgment. After years of rejected truth, God gives the silence people functionally chose. No vision, no word, no guidance — the punishment of spiritual abandonment. | 
False Prophets    | Those who saw "vain visions" and failed to expose sin. They prescribed peace when judgment was coming. Their ministry of comfort without truth directly contributed to national destruction. | 
"Thy Breach Is Great Like the Sea" | Incomparable, oceanic suffering. No human analogy fits. No comforter is adequate. Some grief is so vast that only God can enter it. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "The Lord was AS an enemy" (v.5). Sometimes God's discipline FEELS like
   attack. Don't confuse the sensation with the reality. A surgeon's knife
   and an enemy's blade both cut — but with opposite intentions. Ask: is
   this discipline or attack? If discipline — submit and learn.

2. God "fulfilled His word" (v.17). He does what He says — blessings AND
   curses. If you're living in defiance of clear biblical commands, don't be
   surprised when consequences arrive. God warned. He always warns. He then
   fulfills.

3. The prophets "discovered not thine iniquity" (v.14). Who in your life
   tells you the truth? Surround yourself with people who love you enough to
   expose your sin — not just comfort your ego. False prophets feel nice;
   true prophets save lives.

4. "Her prophets find no vision" (v.9). Is God silent in your life? Could it
   be because you rejected the last word He gave? God sometimes withdraws
   His voice when previous words were ignored. Go back to the LAST thing He
   said. Obey THAT first.

5. "Thy breach is great like the sea" (v.13). If your grief feels oceanic —
   unspeakable, overwhelming, beyond human comfort — Lamentations validates
   that. Some suffering IS incomparable. Don't let anyone minimize your
   pain. But also: the God whose judgment is oceanic has mercy that is
   deeper still (ch.3).

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Lamentations Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/lamentations/

David Guzik — Lamentations 2 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/lamentations-2/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

1. I am the man that hath seen affliction by the rod of his wrath.

3. Surely against me is he turned; he turneth his hand against me all the day.

5. He hath builded against me, and compassed me with gall and travail.
6. He hath set me in dark places, as they that be dead of old.
7. He hath hedged me about, that I cannot get out: he hath made my chain heavy.
8. Also when I cry and shout, he shutteth out my prayer.
9. He hath inclosed my ways with hewn stone, he hath made my paths crooked.
10. He was unto me as a bear lying in wait, and as a lion in secret places.
11. He hath turned aside my ways, and pulled me in pieces: he hath made me desolate.
12. He hath bent his bow, and set me as a mark for the arrow.
13. He hath caused the arrows of his quiver to enter into my reins.
14. I was a derision to all my people; and their song all the day.
15. He hath filled me with bitterness, he hath made me drunken with wormwood.
16. He hath also broken my teeth with gravel stones, he hath covered me with ashes.
17. And thou hast removed my soul far off from peace: I forgat prosperity.
18. And I said, My strength and my hope is perished from the LORD:
19. Remembering mine affliction and my misery, the wormwood and the gall.
20. My soul hath them still in remembrance, and is humbled in me.
21. This I recall to my mind, therefore have I hope.
22. It is of the LORD’S mercies that we are not consumed, because his compassions fail not.

3.notes:
Lamentations - Chapter 3
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The central chapter and theological heart of Lamentations. It begins with the darkest personal lament: "I am the man that hath seen affliction." Then, in the very pit of despair, hope erupts: "It is of the LORD's mercies that we are not consumed, because his compassions fail not. They are new every morning: great is thy faithfulness." The chapter moves from despair to hope to patient waiting to confession — a complete journey through grief to faith.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Traditionally Jeremiah (the "weeping prophet"), written
after Jerusalem's fall in 586 BC. The personal "I" of this
chapter may be Jeremiah himself, or he may voice the experience
of the suffering nation corporately.

Classification: Poetic Lament / Personal Testimony of Hope
Key Themes: Personal affliction, the faithfulness of God even in
judgment, mercy new every morning, the goodness of waiting on
God, hope in the pit of despair, God's sovereignty over both
good and evil, the call to self-examination and return

Historical Context: Chapter 3 is the structural center of
Lamentations — the book has five chapters in an A-B-C-B-A
pattern, with chapter 3 as the pivot. It is also the longest:
a TRIPLE acrostic (66 verses, three verses for each Hebrew
letter). This elaborate structure at the center shows deliberate
theological emphasis: the HOPE message is the focal point.

The chapter's movement is profound: (1) vv.1-18: unrelenting
despair — God has walled me in, broken me, made me desolate;
(2) vv.19-24: "This I recall to mind, therefore have I hope" —
the deliberate CHOICE to remember God's character; (3) vv.25-39:
theological reflection on God's goodness, patience, and
sovereignty; (4) vv.40-54: the call to examine ourselves,
return to God, and cry out; (5) vv.55-66: the personal cry
for deliverance and justice.

The famous lines (vv.22-23) are the wellspring of the hymn
"Great Is Thy Faithfulness" — composed by Thomas Chisholm in
1923. The remarkable thing: these words of hope aren't spoken
from prosperity but from RUINS. Faithfulness proclaimed while
sitting in ashes. This is faith at its most authentic.

Structure:
- The Man of Affliction: God's Rod (vv.1-18)
- "My Strength Is Perished... My Hope" (vv.18-20)
- The Turn: "THIS I Recall — Therefore I Hope" (vv.21-24)
- God's Mercies New Every Morning (vv.22-23)
- The Goodness of Waiting Quietly (vv.25-30)
- God Does Not Willingly Afflict (vv.31-39)
- The Call to Self-Examination and Return (vv.40-42)
- Continuing Lament and Persecution (vv.43-54)
- The Cry for Deliverance (vv.55-66)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.22             | Psalm       | 103     | 8-9                | "The LORD is merciful and gracious... he will not always chide."
v.22             | Malachi     | 3       | 6                  | "For I am the LORD, I change not; therefore ye sons of Jacob are not consumed."
v.23             | Psalm       | 30      | 5                  | "weeping may endure for a night, but joy cometh in the morning."
v.24             | Psalm       | 73      | 26                 | "God is the strength of my heart, and my portion for ever."
v.25             | Isaiah      | 40      | 31                 | "they that wait upon the LORD shall renew their strength."
v.33             | Ezekiel     | 33      | 11                 | "I have no pleasure in the death of the wicked."
v.38             | Isaiah      | 45      | 7                  | "I make peace, and create evil: I the LORD do all these things."
v.40             | 2 Corinthians | 13      | 5                  | "Examine yourselves, whether ye be in the faith."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/lamentations-3/

Charles Spurgeon:
  "Great is thy faithfulness! — said by a man sitting in ruins,
  surrounded by corpses, with a destroyed temple behind him and
  exile before him. THIS is when faithfulness means the most —
  not when the sun shines but when the night is absolute. Anyone
  can praise in prosperity. But the soul that sings 'Great is
  thy faithfulness' from the ashes — THAT soul knows God."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.22             | KJV         | "mercies" (KJV) / "steadfast love" (ESV) — Hebrew "chesed" (plural "chasdey") = covenant love, loyal love, lovingkindness. This is God's covenant commitment — unbreakable, unconditional, rooted in His character not our deserving. It's the reason they "are not consumed."
v.22             | KJV         | "compassions" (KJV/ESV) — Hebrew "rachamim" = deep mercy, womb-love (from "rechem" = womb). The most visceral, maternal mercy — the feeling of a mother for the child of her womb. God's compassion is MATERNAL, instinctive, gut-level.
v.23             | KJV         | "faithfulness" (KJV/ESV) — Hebrew "emunah" = firmness, steadfastness, reliability. From "amen" = to be firm/sure. God's faithfulness is His firmness — He doesn't waver, shift, or change His commitment. It's new every MORNING because He never runs out.
v.33             | KJV         | "willingly" (KJV) / "from his heart" (ESV) — Hebrew "millibbo" = from his heart. Literally: God does not afflict FROM HIS HEART. His heart's desire is mercy; His judgment is necessitated by His justice, not desired by His love. This nuance is crucial: God IS just (He must judge) but His HEART is for mercy.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Chesed (Steadfast Love) | God's covenant loyalty: unfailing, unearned, unconditional. The reason His people are "not consumed." It outlasts their failures and survives their rebellion. The foundation of hope in the ruins. | 
Rachamim (Compassions) | Womb-mercy: the deep, physical, maternal love of God for His people. As instinctive as a mother's love for her newborn. New every morning — never stale, never exhausted. | 
Great Is Thy Faithfulness | God's reliability, spoken from devastation. The inspiration for Thomas Chisholm's 1923 hymn. Faithfulness means the most when spoken from the hardest places. | 
"The LORD Is My Portion" | When all earthly inheritance is gone, God Himself is the inheritance. Priests received no land because "the LORD is their inheritance" (Deuteronomy 18:2). In the ruins, everyone becomes a priest — God alone is enough. | 
Triple Acrostic   | Chapter 3 has THREE verses for each Hebrew letter (66 total). The most elaborate structure in Lamentations — placed at the CENTER. The hope message receives the most emphasis. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "It is of the LORD's mercies that we are not consumed" (v.22). You woke
   up this morning. You're breathing. You exist. That is MERCY. You deserved
   consumption; you received compassion. Start today with gratitude for
   survival, not complaint about circumstances.

2. "They are new every morning" (v.23). God's mercies don't carry over from
   yesterday — they arrive FRESH each day. Yesterday's failures don't
   exhaust today's grace. Whatever you blew yesterday, today is new supply.
   Great is His faithfulness.

3. "The LORD is my portion" (v.24). If everything were taken — health,
   family, career, reputation — would God alone be enough? This is the
   question Lamentations 3 asks. The truest faith says "yes" not from
   comfort but from the ruins.

4. "Let us search and try our ways" (v.40). Before blaming God, blaming
   others, or blaming circumstances — search YOURSELF. Self-examination is
   the missing step between suffering and restoration. What in YOU
   contributed to where you are?

5. "He doth not afflict willingly" (v.33). God's HEART is not in your pain.
   He is not gleeful over your suffering. His heart is for mercy; His
   justice requires discipline. The surgeon doesn't enjoy cutting — but the
   cancer must be removed. Take comfort: His heart weeps with you even as
   His hand corrects.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Lamentations Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/lamentations/

David Guzik — Lamentations 3 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/lamentations-3/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

========================================
Study Notes generated for personal Bible study use.
Translations referenced: KJV, ESV, ASV, NET, WEB
Commentary sources: Enduring Word (enduringword.com), Charles
Spurgeon (Public Domain)

4.ESV:
1. How the gold has grown dim, how the pure gold is changed! The holy stones lie scattered at the head of every street.

3. Even jackals offer the breast; they nurse their young; but the daughter of my people has become cruel, like the ostriches in the wilderness.
4. The tongue of the nursing infant sticks to the roof of its mouth for thirst; the children beg for food, but no one gives to them.
5. Those who once feasted on delicacies perish in the streets; those who were brought up in purple embrace ash heaps.
6. For the chastisement of the daughter of my people has been greater than the punishment of Sodom, which was overthrown in a moment, and no hands were wrung for her.
7. Her princes were purer than snow, whiter than milk; their bodies were more ruddy than coral, the beauty of their form was like sapphire.
8. Now their face is blacker than soot; they are not recognized in the streets; their skin has shriveled on their bones; it has become as dry as wood.
9. Happier were the victims of the sword than the victims of hunger, who wasted away, pierced by lack of the fruits of the field.
10. The hands of compassionate women have boiled their own children; they became their food during the destruction of the daughter of my people.
11. The LORD gave full vent to his wrath; he poured out his hot anger, and he kindled a fire in Zion that consumed its foundations.
12. The kings of the earth did not believe, nor any of the inhabitants of the world, that foe or enemy could enter the gates of Jerusalem.
13. This was for the sins of her prophets and the iniquities of her priests, who shed in the midst of her the blood of the righteous.
14. They wandered, blind, through the streets; they were so defiled with blood that no one was able to touch their garments.
15. “Away! Unclean!” people cried at them. “Away! Away! Do not touch!” So they became fugitives and wanderers; people said among the nations, “They shall stay with us no longer.”

4.notes:
Lamentations - Chapter 4
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The horrors of the siege laid bare in unflinching detail. Gold becomes dim, sacred stones scattered. Children beg for bread and receive none. Compassionate mothers cook their own children — a punishment worse than Sodom's instantaneous destruction. The king (God's "anointed") is captured. The prophets and priests polluted the city with innocent blood. Edom gloats — but her turn is coming.

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Traditionally Jeremiah (the "weeping prophet"), written
after Jerusalem's fall in 586 BC. As an eyewitness present
during the siege, Jeremiah saw these atrocities firsthand — the
starvation, the cannibalism, the capture of King Zedekiah.

Classification: Poetic Lament (Acrostic)
Key Themes: The physical horrors of siege and starvation, the
reversal of all social order, sin's consequences worse than
Sodom, the failure of religious leadership (prophets/priests),
the capture of God's anointed, Edom's coming judgment

Historical Context: The siege of Jerusalem lasted approximately
18 months (January 588 to July 586 BC). Food ran out completely.
2 Kings 25:3 records "there was no bread for the people of the
land." The descriptions here match ancient siege conditions:
once food was gone, social collapse followed rapidly. Jeremiah
himself warned that mothers would eat their children (Jeremiah
19:9) — and it happened.

The comparison to Sodom (v.6) is devastating: Sodom was destroyed
INSTANTLY — fire and brimstone in a moment. Jerusalem's
punishment was PROLONGED: months of starvation, slow death,
watching children waste away day by day. Quick destruction is
mercy compared to extended siege. The punishment was "greater
than Sodom" not in eternal consequence but in earthly duration
of suffering.

King Zedekiah is "the breath of our nostrils, the anointed of
the LORD" (v.20) — captured while fleeing through the walls at
night (2 Kings 25:4-7). His sons were killed before his eyes,
then his eyes were put out — the last thing he saw was his
children's deaths. The poetic language ("breath of our nostrils")
shows how deeply the people depended on royal leadership — and
how devastating its loss.

Structure:
- The Gold Grown Dim: Sacred Things Degraded (vv.1-2)
- Children Starving: Mothers Worse than Jackals (vv.3-4)
- The Pampered Now Desolate (vv.5-6)
- The Nazarites: From Beauty to Horror (vv.7-8)
- Better to Die by Sword than Famine (vv.9-10)
- The LORD Poured Out His Fury (v.11)
- Prophets and Priests: Blood-Guilty (vv.13-16)
- Vain Hopes for Help That Never Came (vv.17-18)
- The Anointed Captured (vv.19-20)
- Edom's Coming Judgment (vv.21-22)

CROSS-REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference
-----------------|-------------|---------|--------------------|-----------
v.1              | Isaiah      | 64      | 11                 | "Our holy and our beautiful house... is burned up with fire."
v.6              | Genesis     | 19      | 24-25              | Sodom's instant destruction by fire — LESS prolonged than Jerusalem's
v.6              | Matthew     | 11      | 23-24              | "It shall be more tolerable for the land of Sodom in the day of judgment, than for thee."
v.10             | Deuteronomy | 28      | 56-57              | The covenant curse predicting mothers eating children during siege
v.10             | 2 Kings     | 6       | 28-29              | The earlier instance of cannibalism during siege in Samaria
v.13             | Jeremiah    | 23      | 11                 | "Both prophet and priest are profane."
v.20             | 2 Kings     | 25      | 4-7                | The historical account of Zedekiah's capture, his sons killed, his eyes put out
v.22             | ?           | ?       | ?                  | Obadiah 10-15 — Edom's judgment for rejoicing over Jerusalem's destruction.

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/lamentations-4/

Charles Spurgeon:
  "Compassionate women boiling their children — this is where
  sin ends. Not where it BEGINS — sin begins with a thought, a
  glance, a whispered 'just this once.' But given enough time,
  enough rebellion, enough hardening — sin arrives HERE. At the
  unthinkable. Let every person who toys with sin look at this
  verse and TREMBLE. This is the road's end."

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "dim" (KJV) / "grown dim" (ESV) — Hebrew "yu'am" = to be darkened, dulled, tarnished. Gold doesn't actually tarnish (it's chemically stable) — so the image is metaphorical: what was bright and precious has LOST ITS GLORY. The city's inherent value is unchanged, but its beauty is hidden under devastation.
v.3              | KJV         | "jackals" (ESV) / "sea monsters" (KJV) — Hebrew "tannim" (some manuscripts: "tannin"). Even WILD ANIMALS nurse their young — but Jerusalem's mothers cannot feed their children. The people have become LESS compassionate than beasts. Sin dehumanizes below the animal.
v.10             | KJV         | "sodden" (KJV) / "boiled" (ESV) — Hebrew "bishlu" = to boil, cook. From "bashal" = to cook thoroughly. The clinical language makes the horror worse: this isn't metaphor but literal description. Cooking their own children for food during extreme famine.
v.20             | KJV         | "the anointed of the LORD" (KJV/ESV) — Hebrew "meshiach YHWH" = the LORD's Messiah/anointed one. The same term used for the coming Messiah is here applied to King Zedekiah. Every Davidic king was a "type" — their failure points forward to the true Anointed One who will never be captured.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
Greater Than Sodom | Jerusalem's punishment exceeds Sodom's because it was PROLONGED rather than instantaneous. Slow starvation is worse than quick destruction. Also: greater privilege (having God's temple) brings greater accountability. | 
Compassionate Mothers | Women who were naturally tender and merciful, driven by extremity to the unthinkable. Sin doesn't just corrupt the wicked — it can distort even the most fundamental instincts of love. This is total depravity manifest. | 
The Anointed Captured | King Zedekiah as "the LORD's anointed" — the one under whose "shadow" they expected protection. His capture represents the failure of all human saviors. Only the TRUE Anointed One (Christ) can never be captured or defeated. | 
Edom's Gloating   | Edom (Esau's descendants, Israel's "brother" nation) celebrated Jerusalem's fall. God promises their reckoning. Those who rejoice over the righteous' suffering will face their own judgment. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "How is the gold become dim!" (v.1). What in your life once shone with
   glory but has become tarnished through neglect or sin? Marriage, calling,
   spiritual passion, integrity — gold can grow dim if not maintained. What
   needs polishing today?

2. Sin's end is unthinkable (v.10). Nobody PLANS to arrive at the worst
   version of themselves. Compassionate women didn't become cannibals
   overnight. It was a progression of desperation caused by earlier choices.
   The time to repent is EARLY — before the siege begins.

3. "Greater than Sodom" (v.6). Greater knowledge = greater accountability.
   You have MORE revelation than ancient Israel (the completed Bible, the
   Holy Spirit, 2,000 years of church history). Your responsibility is
   GREATER. Don't presume on grace.

4. "Under his shadow we shall live" (v.20). What human "anointed one" are
   you depending on for safety? Political leaders, pastors, employers — all
   can be "captured." Only Christ the true Anointed One is uncapturable,
   undefeatable, and permanent.

5. Edom's turn is coming (v.22). If you've been watching others gloat over
   your suffering — God sees. He will address it. You don't need to avenge
   yourself; God will "discover their sins." Wait for His timing. Justice is
   His job.

RESOURCES
------------------------------------
The Bible Project — Lamentations Overview:
  https://bibleproject.com/explore/video/lamentations/

David Guzik — Lamentations 4 Written Commentary:
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/lamentations-4/

BibleProject Podcast:
  https://bibleproject.com/podcast/

8. Servants rule over us. There is no one to deliver us out of their hand.
9. We get our bread at the peril of our lives, because of the sword of the wilderness.
10. Our skin is black like an oven, because of the burning heat of famine.
11. They ravished the women in Zion, the virgins in the cities of Judah.
12. Princes were hanged up by their hands. The faces of elders were not honored.
13. The young men carry millstones. The children stumbled under loads of wood.
14. The elders have ceased from the gate, and the young men from their music.
15. The joy of our heart has ceased. Our dance is turned into mourning.
16. The crown has fallen from our head. Woe to us, for we have sinned!
17. For this our heart is faint. For these things our eyes are dim.
18. For the mountain of Zion, which is desolate. The foxes walk on it.
19. You, Yahweh, remain forever. Your throne is from generation to generation.
20. Why do you forget us forever, and forsake us for so long a time?
21. Turn us to yourself, Yahweh, and we will be turned. Renew our days as of old.
22. But you have utterly rejected us. You are very angry against us.
5.notes:
Lamentations - Chapter 5
Bible Study Notes
========================================

CHAPTER SUMMARY
------------------------------------
The final chapter abandons the acrostic structure and becomes a communal prayer — the entire suffering community crying out together. "Remember, O LORD, what is come upon us." They catalog their losses: inheritance given to strangers, women violated, princes hanged, elders dishonored. The crown is fallen. Joy is gone. The closing plea is both desperate and uncertain: "Turn thou us unto thee, O LORD... renew our days as of old. But thou hast utterly rejected us; thou art very wroth against us."

AUTHORSHIP & HISTORICAL BACKGROUND
------------------------------------
Author: Traditionally Jeremiah (the "weeping prophet"), written
after Jerusalem's fall in 586 BC. This final chapter functions
as a communal prayer offered on behalf of the surviving remnant
— those left in the devastated land or beginning their exile.

Classification: Communal Lament / National Prayer
Key Themes: Communal suffering and corporate prayer, the loss
of inheritance and identity, the consequences falling on the
next generation, longing for restoration, the uncertainty of
whether God will restore, the permanence of God vs. the
transience of human suffering

Historical Context: Unlike chapters 1-4, chapter 5 is NOT an
acrostic — though it still has 22 verses (matching the Hebrew
alphabet count). The abandonment of the strict acrostic form
may represent the final dissolution of order: the people have
lost even the capacity for structured grief. This is raw,
unadorned pleading.

The chapter catalogs the specific losses of the post-destruction
community: their inheritance (land given to foreigners), their
family structure (fatherless, mothers widowed), their basic
resources (paying for water and wood that was once free), their
labor (grinding like slaves), their dignity (women raped, princes
hanged, elders dishonored), and their joy (dancing turned to
mourning).

The ending (vv.21-22) is one of the most debated conclusions in
Scripture. Does it end in faith or despair? "Turn thou us unto
thee... UNLESS thou hast utterly rejected us." The Hebrew can be
read either as conditional ("unless") or emphatic ("surely you
have not"). Jewish liturgical tradition repeats v.21 after v.22
in synagogue readings — refusing to let the book end on
rejection. The ambiguity is itself the point: the people don't
KNOW yet whether God will restore. They can only ask.

Structure:
- "Remember, O LORD" — The Opening Cry (v.1)
- Our Inheritance Given to Strangers (vv.2-3)
- The Cost of Basic Survival (vv.4-7)
- Slaves Rule Over Us (vv.8-10)
- Women Violated, Leaders Dishonored (vv.11-14)
- "The Crown Is Fallen From Our Head" (vv.15-18)
- God's Eternal Throne (v.19)
- The Final Plea: "Turn Us... Renew Our Days" (vv.20-22)

------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Book        | Chapter | Verse of Reference | Reference

v.1              | Psalm       | 74      | 2                  | "Remember thy congregation, which thou hast purchased of old."
v.7              | Exodus      | 20      | 5                  | "visiting the iniquity of the fathers upon the children unto the third and fourth generation."
v.7              | Ezekiel     | 18      | 2                  | "The fathers have eaten sour grapes, and the children's teeth are set on edge."
v.16             | Job         | 19      | 9                  | "He hath stripped me of my glory, and taken the crown from my head."
v.19             | Psalm       | 102     | 12                 | "But thou, O LORD, shalt endure for ever; and thy remembrance unto all generations."
v.19             | Hebrews     | 1       | 11-12              | "They shall perish; but thou remainest."
v.21             | Jeremiah    | 31      | 18                 | "Turn thou me, and I shall be turned; for thou art the LORD my God."
v.21             | Psalm       | 80      | 3                  | "Turn us again, O God, and cause thy face to shine; and we shall be saved."

COMMENTARY REFERENCES
------------------------------------
Enduring Word (David Guzik):
  https://enduringword.com/bible-commentary/lamentations-5/

Charles Spurgeon:
  "'Turn thou us unto thee, O LORD, and we shall be turned.' Here
  is the whole of salvation in a sentence. We cannot turn
  ourselves — we are too broken, too far gone, too deep in ruin.
  But if GOD turns us, we WILL be turned. The sinner's only hope
  is that God will do for him what he cannot do for himself. Cry
  this prayer, broken one: 'Turn ME, Lord, for I cannot turn
  myself.'"

TRANSLATION NOTES
------------------------------------
Verse On Reading | Translation | Wording
-----------------|-------------|--------
v.1              | KJV         | "Remember" (KJV/ESV) — Hebrew "zekor" = remember with action, recall and respond. In Hebrew thought, "remembering" is never merely cognitive — it implies intervention. When God "remembers" Noah (Genesis 8:1), He acts. When God "remembers" His covenant, He delivers. This is a plea for ACTION, not attention.
v.16             | KJV         | "crown" (KJV/ESV) — Hebrew "ateret" = wreath, crown, garland. Could be literal royal crown (the Davidic monarchy ended with Zedekiah's capture) or metaphorical (honor, dignity, divine blessing). Either way: what was ON their head is now ON THE GROUND.
v.21             | KJV         | "Turn thou us" (KJV) / "Restore us to yourself" (ESV) — Hebrew "hashivenu" = cause us to return, bring us back. The verb is CAUSATIVE (hiphil form): the action must come FROM God. They cannot return by their own will — God must do the turning. This is the theology of grace: repentance is divine gift before it is human decision.
v.22             | KJV         | "But thou hast utterly rejected us" (KJV) / "unless you have utterly rejected us" (ESV) — Hebrew "ki im" can mean "but/unless/surely not" — the ambiguity is intentional. The ESV reads it as conditional doubt; the KJV as stark statement. The Hebrew allows both — and the TENSION is the point. The book ends without resolution, without knowing the answer.

GLOSSARY
------------------------------------
Term              | Definition                                                                        | Notes
------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------|------
The Fallen Crown  | The end of royal dignity, national sovereignty, and divine favor. The Davidic dynasty interrupted. The glory departed. What defined them nationally has been stripped away. | 
"Turn Thou Us"    | The causative plea: we cannot repent without Your enabling. Turning back to God is itself God's gift. The broken cannot heal themselves. The dead cannot resurrect themselves. Only God initiates restoration. | KJV term
The Unresolved Ending | Lamentations ends without clear resolution. Unlike most biblical books that conclude with restoration or promise, this ends in uncertainty. The theological honesty is deliberate: some seasons have no neat ending. Faith persists without guarantees. | 
Non-Acrostic Form | Chapter 5 has 22 verses but no alphabetical structure. The form itself communicates: even the capacity for ordered grief has dissolved. This is prayer stripped to its barest essentials. | 

PERSONAL REFLECTION & APPLICATION
------------------------------------
1. "Remember, O LORD" (v.1). Sometimes prayer is simply asking God to LOOK.
   Not to explain, not to fix immediately — just to SEE your pain.
   "Remember" is a valid prayer. God, look at what's happened to me.
   Consider my reproach. See me.

2. "Our fathers sinned, and we bear their iniquities" (v.7). You may be
   carrying consequences of others' choices — family dysfunction,
   generational patterns, inherited poverty or trauma. This is real and
   acknowledged. But v.16 adds: "WE have sinned too." Own what's yours while
   acknowledging what was handed to you.

3. "Thou, O LORD, remainest" (v.19). When everything human has fallen —
   career, health, relationships, nations — God REMAINS. His throne is
   unmoved. Root your identity in what cannot be shaken, not in what has
   already fallen.

4. "Turn thou us... and we shall be turned" (v.21). You cannot generate your
   own spiritual revival. You cannot manufacture repentance by willpower.
   ASK God to turn you. The desire to return is itself evidence that He's
   already working. Cooperate with what He's initiating.

5. The book ends unresolved (v.22). Sometimes faith lives in the tension.
   You don't know how it ends yet. You can't see restoration. The question
   hangs: "Will God restore?" And the answer hasn't come. Faith is
   continuing to pray v.21 even when v.22 feels more likely. Keep asking.
   Keep hoping. The answer will come.
